1. Learning and Evaluating Graph Neural Network Explanations based on Counterfactual and Factual Reasoning (The Web Conference 2022, cites: 151)
   Structural data well exists in Web applications, such as social networks in social media, citation networks in academic websites, and threads data in online forums. Due to the complex topology, it is difficult to process and make use of the rich information within such data. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have shown great advantages on learning representations for structural data. However, the non-transparency of the deep learning models makes it non-trivial to explain and interpret the predictions made by GNNs. Meanwhile, it is also a big challenge to evaluate the GNN explanations, since in many cases, the ground-truth explanations are unavailable. In this paper, we take insights of Counterfactual and Factual (CF2) reasoning from causal inference theory, to solve both the learning and evaluation problems in explainable GNNs. For generating explanations, we propose a model-agnostic framework by formulating an optimization problem based on both of the two casual perspectives. This distinguishes CF2 from previous explainable GNNs that only consider one of them. Another contribution of the work is the evaluation of GNN explanations. For quantitatively evaluating the generated explanations without the requirement of ground-truth, we design metrics based on Counterfactual and Factual reasoning to evaluate the necessity and sufficiency of the explanations. Experiments show that no matter ground-truth explanations are available or not, CF2 generates better explanations than previous state-of-the-art methods on real-world datasets. Moreover, the statistic analysis justifies the correlation between the performance on ground-truth evaluation and our proposed metrics.

2. On the Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency of Explaining Graph Neural Networks: A Lower Bound Optimization Approach (Neural Networks 2022, cites: 14)
   The explainability of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) is critical to various GNN applications, yet it remains a significant challenge. A convincing explanation should be both necessary and sufficient simultaneously. However, existing GNN explaining approaches focus on only one of the two aspects, necessity or sufficiency, or a heuristic trade-off between the two. Theoretically, the Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS) holds the potential to identify the most necessary and sufficient explanation since it can mathematically quantify the necessity and sufficiency of an explanation. Nevertheless, the difficulty of obtaining PNS due to non-monotonicity and the challenge of counterfactual estimation limit its wide use. To address the non-identifiability of PNS, we resort to a lower bound of PNS that can be optimized via counterfactual estimation, and propose a framework of Necessary and Sufficient Explanation for GNN (NSEG) via optimizing that lower bound. Specifically, we depict the GNN as a structural causal model (SCM), and estimate the probability of counterfactual via the intervention under the SCM. Additionally, we leverage continuous masks with a sampling strategy to optimize the lower bound to enhance the scalability. Empirical results demonstrate that NSEG outperforms state-of-the-art methods, consistently generating the most necessary and sufficient explanations. The implementation of our NSEG is available at https://github.com/EthanChu7/NSEG.

3. Explainable Artificial Intelligence: Counterfactual Explanations for Risk-Based Decision-Making in Construction (IEEE transactions on engineering management 2024, cites: 20)
   Artificial intelligence (AI) approaches, such as deep learning models, are increasingly used to determine risks in construction. However, the black-box nature of AI models makes their inner workings difficult to understand and interpret. Deploying explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) can help explain why and how the output of AI models is generated. This article addresses the following research question: How can we accurately identify the critical factors influencing tunnel-induced ground settlement and provide counterfactual explanations to support risk-based decision-making? We apply an XAI approach using counterfactual explanations to help understand decision-making surrounding risks when considering control ground settlement. Our approach consists of a: 1) construction of Kernel principal components analysis-based deep neural network (DNN) model; 2) generation of counterfactual explanations; 3) analysis of risk prediction and assessment factors' importance, necessity, and sufficiency. We apply our approach to the San-yang road tunnel project in Wuhan, China. The results demonstrate that the KPCA-DNN model better predicted ground settlement based on high-dimensional input features than the baseline model (i.e., AdaBoost and RandomForest). The bubble chamber pressure→ cutter-head speed→ equipment inclination is also identified as the primary risk path. Our findings indicate that using counterfactual explanations enables transparency and trust in AI-based risk models to be acquired. Moreover, our approach can help site managers, engineers, and tunnel-boring machine operators understand how to manage better and mitigate the risk of ground settlement.

4. Identifying Evidence Subgraphs for Financial Risk Detection via Graph Counterfactual and Factual Reasoning (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Company financial risks pose a significant threat to personal wealth and national economic stability, stimulating increasing attention towards the development of efficient andtimely methods for monitoring them. Current approaches tend to use graph neural networks (GNNs) to model the momentum spillover effect of risks. However, due to the black-box nature of GNNs, these methods leave much to be improved for precise and reliable explanations towards company risks. In this paper, we propose CF3, a novel Counterfactual and Factual learning method for company Financial risk detection, which generates evidence subgraphs on company knowledge graphs to reliably detect and explain company financial risks. Specifically, we first propose a meta-path attribution process based on Granger causality, selecting the meta-paths most relevant to the target node labels to construct an attribution subgraph. Subsequently, we propose anedge-type-aware graph generator to identify important edges, and we also devise a layer-based feature masker to recognize crucial node features. Finally, we utilize counterfactual-factual reasoning and a loss function based on attribution subgraphs to jointly guide the learning of the graph generator and feature masker. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our method compared to state-of-the-art approaches in the field of financial risk detection.

5. UM-Explainer: An Explainability Method for Unsupervised Models Based on Factual and Counterfactual Reasoning (IEEE International Conference on High Performance Computing and Communications 2024, cites: 0)
   Graph neural networks (GNNs) have shown significant advantages in learning structured data representations and have become one of the important methods in machine learning. However, the inherent opacity of deep learning models hinders their widespread application in critical domains, making the explanation of GNNs predictions particularly crucial. While explanations for supervised learning models have been extensively studied, research on generating explanations for unsupervised learning models remains relatively scarce. Current explanation methods for unsupervised models mostly focus on either factual reasoning or counterfactual reasoning. But relying on a single reasoning approach often results in explanations that are either redundant or incomplete. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a novel explanation method for Unsupervised Models named UM-Explainer, which is based on factual and counter-factual reasoning. By calculating the distance between cluster centers and the example to be explained, a contrastive loss based on both factual and counterfactual reasoning is constructed to optimize the mask, thereby generating an ideal explanatory subgraph. To validate the effectiveness of UM-Explainer, we conducted experiments with synthetic and real datasets on multiple unsupervised models. Experimental results demonstrate that UM-Explainer performs outstandingly in generating accurate explanations.

   Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are a technique for sparse decomposition of neural network activations into human-interpretable features. However, current SAEs suffer from feature absorption, where specialized features capture instances of general features creating representation holes, and feature composition, where independent features merge into composite representations. In this work, we introduce Orthogonal SAE (OrtSAE), a novel approach aimed to mitigate these issues by enforcing orthogonality between the learned features. By implementing a new training procedure that penalizes high pairwise cosine similarity between SAE features, OrtSAE promotes the development of disentangled features while scaling linearly with the SAE size, avoiding significant computational overhead. We train OrtSAE across different models and layers and compare it with other methods. We find that OrtSAE discovers 9% more distinct features, reduces feature absorption (by 65%) and composition (by 15%), improves performance on spurious correlation removal (+6%), and achieves on-par performance for other downstream tasks compared to traditional SAEs.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Learned representations are opaque and hard to debug or edit;
how can we reverse-engineer what specific circuits compute,
decompose representations into human-interpretable features,
and verify those decompositions?


### Proposed method
We propose a circuit rationalization and verification framework that treats a model’s internal components (e.g., attention heads, MLP channels, neurons, or edges) as a pool from which a minimal, task-sufficient subcircuit is softly selected. A separator network observes the current sample’s intermediate activations and outputs a sparse, differentiable mask over components. Using these masks, we decompose the representation into two pooled summaries: a rationale summary (the masked features) and an environment summary (the complement). A lightweight predictor is trained to reproduce the model’s original output from the rationale summary alone, with an explicit size penalty on the mask to encourage minimality. Training alternates between optimizing the separator (to find smaller, more faithful rationales) and the predictor (to fit the target), so that the rationale converges to a compact, self-sufficient subcircuit.

To causally test that the separator has isolated the true mechanism, we augment data by recombining subcircuits across a batch: we pair a rationale from sample i with the environment from sample j and require the predictor’s output to follow sample i’s label. This rationale–environment mixing stress-tests whether the selected subcircuit is minimally sufficient and environment-invariant. In parallel, for each output token or logit, we learn a per-output soft intervention mask using a factual/counterfactual objective: under the factual mask the output should remain unchanged, while under the counterfactual (inverted or shuffled) mask it should diverge. This yields necessity–sufficiency curves that quantify how much of the behavior the subcircuit accounts for. For attention-heavy models, we additionally compute a token-level alignment-path score over layers to verify that information flow along attention paths used by the subcircuit is monotonic and consistent with plausible input→output alignments.

To make the subcircuit’s state human-interpretable, we introduce K feature slots over the rationale summary with sparse routing and max-aggregation, so distinct slots preferentially capture different semantic aspects. We train simple probe heads from these slots to regress or classify human-understandable attributes relevant to the domain (e.g., lexical tags for text, physical parameters, or object/region identities for vision); where grounded evidence is available (e.g., linked segments, spans, or parts), we add weak supervision aligning masks to evidence and constrain attention paths that feed the subcircuit to pass through those supports. This produces a dictionary of features whose activations are locally causal (by construction), slot-separated (by sparse routing and max aggregation), and semantically named (by probes and optional groundings).

For external verification and editability, we distill the model’s decision restricted to the rationale summary into a compact student (e.g., gradient-boosted trees) and use exact attributions in the student to cross-check feature importance against our masks and probes. We also harvest implicit interventions by evaluating the same inputs under multiple stochastic or fine-tuned model variants, forming interventional pairs that let us estimate each component’s context-specific causal effect while controlling for confounders. Finally, we support surgical edits by gating or fine-tuning only the parameters feeding into masked components or feature slots, while monitoring off-target drift. Edits can up/down-weight specific slots, swap in alternative slot activations, or clamp attention-path segments; we verify edits with the same sufficiency/comprehensiveness curves, recombination tests, alignment-path scores, and student-model fidelity.

Evaluation reports: (1) sufficiency and comprehensiveness as a function of mask sparsity; (2) recombination accuracy that the rationale determines predictions when mixed with arbitrary environments; (3) alignment-path monotonicity and consistency across layers for attention circuits; (4) agreement with grounded evidence where available; (5) student fidelity and SHAP-based attribution alignment with separator masks; and (6) edit locality, measuring target-behavior change vs. non-target preservation.

### Rationale
The separator-and-augmentation core isolates minimal, self-sufficient subcircuits by construction, then falsifies spurious explanations by mixing rationales with unrelated environments. The factual/counterfactual objective provides robust necessity–sufficiency guarantees for the same mask, while an alignment-path score checks that attention-mediated signal flow adheres to plausible input–output alignments rather than exploiting distributed shortcuts. Slotting and max aggregation encourage the rationale’s state to decompose into a small set of disentangled, human-nameable features, and lightweight probes ground those features in domain semantics without retraining the backbone.

Independent verification closes the loop: a student model affords exact, fast attributions to compare with learned masks, and harvested interventions across model variants estimate context-dependent causal effects without intrusive randomized ablations. Because edits are restricted to the gated subcircuit, users can change behavior predictably with minimal collateral impact, and the same causal and alignment tests certify that the decomposition remains accurate after editing.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [ROBOTICS] SwingBot: Learning Physical Features from In-hand Tactile Exploration for Dynamic Swing-up Manipulation (2101.11812)
   problem: Learned embeddings should be interpretable and transferable, enabling recovery of explicit physical parameters that drive task dynamics.
   method:  With the fusion encoder frozen, a lightweight MLP head regresses mass, center of mass, and moment of inertia, and classifies friction from the learned embedding. Analysis shows complementary strengths of the two explorations on unseen objects—tilting excels at mass and COM (e.g., mass error 0.184 vs 0.263 for shaking), while shaking excels at friction (90.1% vs 75.6% for tilting); the combined embedding balances properties and improves friction to 93.9%. PCA visualizations further show the embedding is task-oriented: objects with similar swing-up policy–angle distributions cluster together, supporting generalization and policy reuse.

2. [CV] Graph Rationalization with Environment-based Augmentations (2206.02886)
   problem: Explicitly decoding/encoding rationale and augmented subgraphs is computationally prohibitive and mixing subgraphs across graphs is technically difficult, which limits scalability and coherence of learned rationales.
   method:  Propose GREA, a latent-space framework that avoids explicit subgraph construction. A separator (GNN1+MLP1) produces node-level rationale probabilities m; a second encoder (GNN2) yields contextual node embeddings H. Rationale and environment graph representations are computed via masked pooling (h^(r)=Σ(m⊙H), h^(e)=Σ((1−m)⊙H)), and augmented representations are formed by aggregating h^(r)_i with h^(e)_j across the batch. Training alternates between separator and predictor, includes a size regularizer L_reg to control rationale size, and uses rationale-only predictions at inference, yielding higher efficiency and accuracy.

3. [ROBOTICS] Bayesian Imitation Learning for End-to-End Mobile Manipulation (2202.07600)
   problem: Policies learned end-to-end are often black boxes, making it hard to understand which inputs matter or when the model is uncertain.
   method:  Leverage VIB’s learned rates and latent structure for explainability: higher rates indicate inputs that require more information to act accurately. In door opening, rates peak during the task-critical latch manipulation phase for both modalities, while being low during base-only navigation, and nearest-neighbor analysis in latent space retrieves sim and real images from the same task phase, evidencing domain-agnostic, action-relevant representations. These properties provide interpretable diagnostics for model behavior and sensor reliability.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] End-to-End Transformer Based Model for Image Captioning (2203.15350)
   problem: In Transformer-based captioning, vision–language interaction typically occurs only at cross-attention, leaving the decoder without global visual context early in processing, which weakens reasoning over the image.
   method:  PureT introduces a pre-fusion module in every decoder block that concatenates the refined global visual feature with previously generated word embeddings before masked self-attention, providing an early inter-modal interaction. This is followed by language masked self-attention and then cross-attention over the refined grid features. Ablation studies show the pre-fusion consistently improves all metrics compared to variants without it.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Visual Semantic Reasoning for Image-Text Matching (1909.02701)
   problem: Pure metric learning may not sufficiently enforce that the learned image embeddings encode captionable semantics needed for robust cross-modal alignment.
   method:  Jointly optimize a hinge-based triplet ranking loss with hard negatives in the shared embedding space (using simple inner-product similarity) together with an auxiliary sequence-to-sequence caption generation loss with attention on the same visual features. This multi-objective training encourages embeddings that are both discriminative for retrieval and predictive of captions, improving R@1 over training without the generation objective.

7. [CV] Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (2107.11787)
   problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation trained with only image-level labels relies on sparse CAM seeds and off-the-shelf saliency maps that are used offline and not adapted to the target data, yielding low-quality pseudo labels and coarse boundaries.
   method:  AuxSegNet is a joint multi-task framework that treats semantic segmentation as the primary task and leverages multi-label image classification and saliency detection as auxiliary tasks, all sharing a backbone and trained end-to-end using only image-level labels and pre-trained saliency maps as initial pseudo labels. The classification branch produces CAMs; the saliency and segmentation branches use atrous conv decoders and are trained with losses on both initial and refined predictions against pseudo labels. Saliency pseudo labels are updated online by fusing the model’s refined saliency with pre-trained saliency through a dense CRF, while segmentation pseudo labels are generated from CAMs (and later their affinity-refined versions), enabling the auxiliary tasks to adapt to the target dataset and improve segmentation performance iteratively.

8. [CV] ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer (2203.10790)
   problem: Designing a general-purpose vision backbone that balances accuracy and efficiency across classification, detection, and segmentation while modeling long-range dependencies without deep stacking.
   method:  ScalableViT is a hierarchical ViT backbone that alternates IW-MSA and SSA blocks within each stage and inserts a positional encoding generator (PEG) at the front of stages. This topology leverages SSA’s context-preserving scaling and IWSA’s inter-window interaction to capture long-range dependencies in single layers, improving the accuracy–cost trade-off. Empirically, ScalableViT-S improves ImageNet-1K top-1 by +1.4% over Twins-SVT-S and +1.8% over Swin-T at similar FLOPs, and yields substantial gains on COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation.

9. [CV] Real-time Hyperspectral Imaging in Hardware via Trained Metasurface Encoders (2204.02084)
   problem: Fixed filter banks cannot optimally capture task-specific spectral features for downstream vision tasks like semantic segmentation, especially when RGB cues are ambiguous (e.g., real vs. artificial fruits with similar colors).
   method:  Develop a differentiable, physics-aware encoder–decoder framework (d-ALFRED) that jointly optimizes metasurface projector responses and geometry with the downstream decoder via backpropagation. The encoder is modeled by coupled-mode theory (with learnable resonance parameters mapped from geometry), enabling gradient flow through the metasurface transfer function; a U-Net decoder is trained end-to-end for pixel-wise classification. The learned spectral encoders yield compact barcodes that preserve discriminative cues, achieving mIoU ~0.81 (simulation) and ~0.74 (fabricated devices), outperforming an RGB-only baseline (~0.68 mIoU) and significantly improving IoU/F1 on challenging real–artificial fruit pairs.

10. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

11. [MULTIMODAL] BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps (2005.04625)
   problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
   method:  Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

12. [RL] XRL-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating and Comparing Explainable Reinforcement Learning Techniques (2402.12685)
   problem: Interpreting deep RL policies on high-dimensional tabular states is challenging; gradient- or perturbation-based explainers can be unstable, slow, or misaligned with the agent’s decision logic.
   method:  TabularSHAP trains an ensemble tree student model (e.g., LightGBM) on state–action pairs generated by the DRL policy to mimic its state→action mapping, then applies TreeSHAP to compute optimal local SHAP attributions for actions. On XRL-Bench, TabularSHAP attains top AIM/AUM fidelity in 7/8 tabular evaluations across datasets, demonstrates commendable stability, and is highly efficient (e.g., ~0.003s per sample on large tabular states), enabling faithful and fast explanations of RL decision-making.

13. [IR_REC] Continuous Input Embedding Size Search For Recommender Systems (2304.03501)
   problem: Recommender systems typically assign a uniform, large embedding size to all users/items, which is memory-inefficient. Prior RL-based embedding-size search operates over small, discrete action sets, limiting granularity and often yielding suboptimal accuracy–memory trade-offs under fixed budgets.
   method:  CIESS formulates per-user/item embedding size selection as a continuous-action RL problem using an actor–critic (TD3) framework with separate user/item actors. It enforces chosen sizes via a binary mask over a max-size embedding table, and designs states that include normalized popularity, current size, and a quality-ratio indicator capturing accuracy drop vs. full-size. The reward blends ranking quality with a squared memory-cost term (scaled by λ) to encourage fast early pruning and fine-grained later adjustments. The approach is model-agnostic and plugs into latent factor recommenders.

14. [IR_REC] Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation (1811.01802)
   problem: Unbiased learning-to-rank from clicks requires accurate examination-bias (propensity) estimates, but existing methods are restricted to the Position-Based Model (PBM) that ignores query/user context and often rely on intrusive randomized swaps or difficult document-level relevance modeling.
   method:  Introduce the Contextual Position-Based Model (CPBM), which models examination as Pr(E=1 | k, x) depending on rank k and context x, and learn it via a neural network h(k, x). Develop an AllPairs estimator that harvests implicit interventions from logs of at least two historic rankers (e.g., A/B tests or stationary deployments): define interventional sets S_{k,k'} of (x, d) that appear at both positions under different rankers, compute assignment weights q_k(x, d), form IPS-weighted click labels, and jointly fit h and an average-relevance model g(k, k', x) by maximizing a weighted cross-entropy objective.    method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

They propose the Alignment Path Score (APS), which aggregates alignment-path similarity across tokens and layers to quantify fine-grained alignment quality. APS exhibits stronger linear correlation with the modality gap than sequence-level metrics (e.g., higher R^2 under LoRA), and alignment paths show high monotonicity and consistency across models (e.g., Spearman ≈ 0.85 for cosine), indicating token-level alignment is a key mechanism underlying LSLM speech understanding.

1. EXP-CAM: Explanation Generation and Circuit Discovery Using Classifier Activation Matching ( 2025, cites: 0)
   Machine learning models, by virtue of training, learn a large repertoire of decision rules for any given input, and any one of these may suffice to justify a prediction. However, in high-dimensional input spaces, such rules are difficult to identify and interpret. In this paper, we introduce EXP-CAM: an explanation generation and circuit discovery approach using Classifier Activation Matching. EXP-CAM can generate minimal and faithful explanations for the decisions of pre-trained image classifiers that not only preserve the model's decision but are also concise and human-readable. We aim to identify minimal explanations that not only preserve the model's decision but are also concise and human-readable. To achieve this, we train a lightweight auto-encoder to produce binary masks that learns to highlight the decision-wise critical regions of an image while discarding irrelevant background. The training objective integrates activation alignment across multiple layers, consistency at the output label, priors that encourage sparsity, and compactness, along with a robustness constraint that enforces faithfulness. The minimal explanations so generated also lead us to mechanistically interpreting the model internals. In this regard we also introduce a circuit readout procedure wherein using the explanation's forward pass and gradients, we identify active channels and construct a channel-level graph, scoring inter-layer edges by ingress weight magnitude times source activation and feature-to-class links by classifier weight magnitude times feature activation. Together, these contributions provide a practical bridge between minimal input-level explanations and a mechanistic understanding of the internal computations driving model decisions.

   Graph rationalization methods aim to improve the explainability of Graph Neural Networks by identifying critical subgraphs (rationales) for task prediction. Motivated by increasing concerns over data privacy, federated graph rationalization has recently gained traction as a novel research area. However, in federated settings, data heterogeneity across clients exacerbates shortcut learning, where models rely on spurious and client-specific features rather than invariant causal rationales. Existing solutions, such as environment-aware data augmentation, suffer from low-quality environment representations. To address this, we propose DiffGR, a Diff erence-based sample selection strategy for federated Graph Rationalization. DiffGR selects samples where local and global models exhibit the highest prediction discrepancies, as these likely reflect strong shortcut reliance, enabling more accurate environment representations. Additionally, we introduce a mutual information (MI) inspired environment-conditioned data augmentation method that minimizes MI between environments and predictions while maximizing MI between rationales and predictions. Experiments on real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of DiffGR in improving rationale quality and model robustness in federated settings.    Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) perform effectively when training and testing graphs are drawn from the same distribution, but struggle to generalize well in the face of distribution shifts. To address this issue, existing mainstreaming graph rationalization methods first identify rationale and environment subgraphs from input graphs, and then diversify training distributions by augmenting the environment subgraphs. However, these methods merely combine the learned rationale subgraphs with environment subgraphs in the representation space to produce augmentation samples, failing to produce sufficiently diverse distributions. Thus, in this paper, we propose to achieve an effective Graph Rationalization by Boosting Environmental diversity, a GRBE approach that generates the augmented samples in the original graph space to improve the diversity of the environment subgraph. Secondly, to ensure the diversity of augmented samples, we propose an environment diversity augmentation strategy in GRBE that mixes the environment subgraphs of different graphs in the original graph space and then combines the new environment subgraphs with rationale subgraphs to generate augmented graphs. The average improvements of 7.65% and 6.11% in rationalization and classification performance on benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of GRBE over state-of-the-art approaches.

This information is used to generate counterfactual samples for local clients, enhancing the robustness of task predictions. Graph rationalization can enhance the model performance because the mapping between the graph rationale and the prediction label is viewed as invariant, by definition. To ensure the discriminative power of the extracted rationale subgraphs, a key technique named intervention is applied, whose core idea is that given changing environment subgraphs, the semantics from the rationale subgraph is invariant, which guarantees the correct prediction result. However, most, if not all, of the existing graph rationalization methods develop their intervention strategies on the graph level, which is coarse-grained. Our experiments involve 7 real-world datasets, and the proposed FIG shows significant performance advantages compared to 13 baseline methods.

To address these problems, we propose a novel causality-aligned structure rationalization (CASR) scheme to construct invariant rationales by probing the coherent and causal patterns, which facilitates GNN models to make stable and reliable predictions in case of adversarial biased perturbations. Specifically, the initial graph samples across domains are leveraged to boost the diversity of datasets and perceive the interaction between shortcuts. Subsequently, the causal invariant rationales can be obtained during the interventions. This allows the GNN model to extrapolate risk variations from a single observed environment to multiple unknown environments. Moreover, the query feedback mechanism can progressively promote the consistency-driven optimal rationalization by reinforcing real essences and eliminating spurious shortcuts. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our scheme against adversarial biased perturbations from data manipulation attacks and out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts on various graph-structured datasets. Notably, we reveal that the capture of distinctive rationales can greatly reduce the dependence on shortcut cues and improve the robustness of OOD generalization.

1. Learning and Evaluating Graph Neural Network Explanations based on Counterfactual and Factual Reasoning (The Web Conference 2022, cites: 151)
   Structural data well exists in Web applications, such as social networks in social media, citation networks in academic websites, and threads data in online forums. Due to the complex topology, it is difficult to process and make use of the rich information within such data. However, the non-transparency of the deep learning models makes it non-trivial to explain and interpret the predictions made by GNNs. Meanwhile, it is also a big challenge to evaluate the GNN explanations, since in many cases, the ground-truth explanations are unavailable. In this paper, we take insights of Counterfactual and Factual (CF2) reasoning from causal inference theory, to solve both the learning and evaluation problems in explainable GNNs. For generating explanations, we propose a model-agnostic framework by formulating an optimization problem based on both of the two casual perspectives. This distinguishes CF2 from previous explainable GNNs that only consider one of them. Another contribution of the work is the evaluation of GNN explanations. For quantitatively evaluating the generated explanations without the requirement of ground-truth, we design metrics based on Counterfactual and Factual reasoning to evaluate the necessity and sufficiency of the explanations. Experiments show that no matter ground-truth explanations are available or not, CF2 generates better explanations than previous state-of-the-art methods on real-world datasets. Moreover, the statistic analysis justifies the correlation between the performance on ground-truth evaluation and our proposed metrics.

2. On the Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency of Explaining Graph Neural Networks: A Lower Bound Optimization Approach (Neural Networks 2022, cites: 14)
   The explainability of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) is critical to various GNN applications, yet it remains a significant challenge. A convincing explanation should be both necessary and sufficient simultaneously. However, existing GNN explaining approaches focus on only one of the two aspects, necessity or sufficiency, or a heuristic trade-off between the two. Theoretically, the Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS) holds the potential to identify the most necessary and sufficient explanation since it can mathematically quantify the necessity and sufficiency of an explanation. Nevertheless, the difficulty of obtaining PNS due to non-monotonicity and the challenge of counterfactual estimation limit its wide use. To address the non-identifiability of PNS, we resort to a lower bound of PNS that can be optimized via counterfactual estimation, and propose a framework of Necessary and Sufficient Explanation for GNN (NSEG) via optimizing that lower bound. Specifically, we depict the GNN as a structural causal model (SCM), and estimate the probability of counterfactual via the intervention under the SCM. Additionally, we leverage continuous masks with a sampling strategy to optimize the lower bound to enhance the scalability. Empirical results demonstrate that NSEG outperforms state-of-the-art methods, consistently generating the most necessary and sufficient explanations. The implementation of our NSEG is available at https://github.com/EthanChu7/NSEG.

3. Explainable Artificial Intelligence: Counterfactual Explanations for Risk-Based Decision-Making in Construction (IEEE transactions on engineering management 2024, cites: 20)
   Artificial intelligence (AI) approaches, such as deep learning models, are increasingly used to determine risks in construction. However, the black-box nature of AI models makes their inner workings difficult to understand and interpret. Deploying explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) can help explain why and how the output of AI models is generated. This article addresses the following research question: How can we accurately identify the critical factors influencing tunnel-induced ground settlement and provide counterfactual explanations to support risk-based decision-making? We apply an XAI approach using counterfactual explanations to help understand decision-making surrounding risks when considering control ground settlement. Our approach consists of a: 1) construction of Kernel principal components analysis-based deep neural network (DNN) model; 2) generation of counterfactual explanations; 3) analysis of risk prediction and assessment factors' importance, necessity, and sufficiency. We apply our approach to the San-yang road tunnel project in Wuhan, China. The results demonstrate that the KPCA-DNN model better predicted ground settlement based on high-dimensional input features than the baseline model (i.e., AdaBoost and RandomForest). The bubble chamber pressure→ cutter-head speed→ equipment inclination is also identified as the primary risk path. Our findings indicate that using counterfactual explanations enables transparency and trust in AI-based risk models to be acquired. Moreover, our approach can help site managers, engineers, and tunnel-boring machine operators understand how to manage better and mitigate the risk of ground settlement.

1. OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 3)
   Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are a technique for sparse decomposition of neural network activations into human-interpretable features. However, current SAEs suffer from feature absorption, where specialized features capture instances of general features creating representation holes, and feature composition, where independent features merge into composite representations. In this work, we introduce Orthogonal SAE (OrtSAE), a novel approach aimed to mitigate these issues by enforcing orthogonality between the learned features. By implementing a new training procedure that penalizes high pairwise cosine similarity between SAE features, OrtSAE promotes the development of disentangled features while scaling linearly with the SAE size, avoiding significant computational overhead. We train OrtSAE across different models and layers and compare it with other methods. We find that OrtSAE discovers 9% more distinct features, reduces feature absorption (by 65%) and composition (by 15%), improves performance on spurious correlation removal (+6%), and achieves on-par performance for other downstream tasks compared to traditional SAEs.

1. Resurrecting the Salmon: Rethinking Mechanistic Interpretability with Domain-Specific Sparse Autoencoders (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose large language model (LLM) activations into latent features that reveal mechanistic structure. Conventional SAEs train on broad data distributions, forcing a fixed latent budget to capture only high-frequency, generic patterns. This often results in significant linear ``dark matter''in reconstruction error and produces latents that fragment or absorb each other, complicating interpretation. We show that restricting SAE training to a well-defined domain (medical text) reallocates capacity to domain-specific features, improving both reconstruction fidelity and interpretability. Training JumpReLU SAEs on layer-20 activations of Gemma-2 models using 195k clinical QA examples, we find that domain-confined SAEs explain up to 20\% more variance, achieve higher loss recovery, and reduce linear residual error compared to broad-domain SAEs. Automated and human evaluations confirm that learned features align with clinically meaningful concepts (e.g., ``taste sensations''or ``infectious mononucleosis''), rather than frequent but uninformative tokens. These domain-specific SAEs capture relevant linear structure, leaving a smaller, more purely nonlinear residual. We conclude that domain-confinement mitigates key limitations of broad-domain SAEs, enabling more complete and interpretable latent decompositions, and suggesting the field may need to question ``foundation-model''scaling for general-purpose SAEs.

2. MIB: A Mechanistic Interpretability Benchmark (International Conference on Machine Learning 2025, cites: 22)
   How can we know whether new mechanistic interpretability methods achieve real improvements? In pursuit of lasting evaluation standards, we propose MIB, a Mechanistic Interpretability Benchmark, with two tracks spanning four tasks and five models. MIB favors methods that precisely and concisely recover relevant causal pathways or causal variables in neural language models. The circuit localization track compares methods that locate the model components - and connections between them - most important for performing a task (e.g., attribution patching or information flow routes). The causal variable localization track compares methods that featurize a hidden vector, e.g., sparse autoencoders (SAEs) or distributed alignment search (DAS), and align those features to a task-relevant causal variable. Using MIB, we find that attribution and mask optimization methods perform best on circuit localization. For causal variable localization, we find that the supervised DAS method performs best, while SAE features are not better than neurons, i.e., non-featurized hidden vectors. These findings illustrate that MIB enables meaningful comparisons, and increases our confidence that there has been real progress in the field.

3. Route Sparse Autoencoder to Interpret Large Language Models (Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2025, cites: 20)
   Mechanistic interpretability of large language models (LLMs) aims to uncover the internal processes of information propagation and reasoning. Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have demonstrated promise in this domain by extracting interpretable and monosemantic features. However, prior works primarily focus on feature extraction from a single layer, failing to effectively capture activations that span multiple layers. In this paper, we introduce Route Sparse Autoencoder (RouteSAE), a new framework that integrates a routing mechanism with a shared SAE to efficiently extract features from multiple layers. It dynamically assigns weights to activations from different layers, incurring minimal parameter overhead while achieving high interpretability and flexibility for targeted feature manipulation. We evaluate RouteSAE through extensive experiments on Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct. Specifically, under the same sparsity constraint of 64, RouteSAE extracts 22.5% more features than baseline SAEs while achieving a 22.3% higher interpretability score. These results underscore the potential of RouteSAE as a scalable and effective method for LLM interpretability, with applications in feature discovery and model intervention. Our codes are available at https://github.com/swei2001/RouteSAEs.

4. Binary Autoencoder for Mechanistic Interpretability of Large Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Existing works are dedicated to untangling atomized numerical components (features) from the hidden states of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, they typically rely on autoencoders constrained by some training-time regularization on single training instances, without an explicit guarantee of global sparsity among instances, causing a large amount of dense (simultaneously inactive) features, harming the feature sparsity and atomization. In this paper, we propose a novel autoencoder variant that enforces minimal entropy on minibatches of hidden activations, thereby promoting feature independence and sparsity across instances. For efficient entropy calculation, we discretize the hidden activations to 1-bit via a step function and apply gradient estimation to enable backpropagation, so that we term it as Binary Autoencoder (BAE) and empirically demonstrate two major applications: (1) Feature set entropy calculation. Entropy can be reliably estimated on binary hidden activations, which can be leveraged to characterize the inference dynamics of LLMs. (2) Feature untangling. Compared to typical methods, due to improved training strategy, BAE avoids dense features while producing the largest number of interpretable ones among baselines.

5. Towards Understanding the Nature of Attention with Low-Rank Sparse Decomposition (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)
   We propose Low-Rank Sparse Attention (Lorsa), a sparse replacement model of Transformer attention layers to disentangle original Multi Head Self Attention (MHSA) into individually comprehensible components. Lorsa is designed to address the challenge of attention superposition to understand attention-mediated interaction between features in different token positions. We show that Lorsa heads find cleaner and finer-grained versions of previously discovered MHSA behaviors like induction heads, successor heads and attention sink behavior (i.e., heavily attending to the first token). Lorsa and Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) are both sparse dictionary learning methods applied to different Transformer components, and lead to consistent findings in many ways. For instance, we discover a comprehensive family of arithmetic-specific Lorsa heads, each corresponding to an atomic operation in Llama-3.1-8B. Automated interpretability analysis indicates that Lorsa achieves parity with SAE in interpretability while Lorsa exhibits superior circuit discovery properties, especially for features computed collectively by multiple MHSA heads. We also conduct extensive experiments on architectural design ablation, Lorsa scaling law and error analysis.

6. Dictionary Learning Improves Patch-Free Circuit Discovery in Mechanistic Interpretability: A Case Study on Othello-GPT (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 26)
   Sparse dictionary learning has been a rapidly growing technique in mechanistic interpretability to attack superposition and extract more human-understandable features from model activations. We ask a further question based on the extracted more monosemantic features: How do we recognize circuits connecting the enormous amount of dictionary features? We propose a circuit discovery framework alternative to activation patching. Our framework suffers less from out-of-distribution and proves to be more efficient in terms of asymptotic complexity. The basic unit in our framework is dictionary features decomposed from all modules writing to the residual stream, including embedding, attention output and MLP output. Starting from any logit, dictionary feature or attention score, we manage to trace down to lower-level dictionary features of all tokens and compute their contribution to these more interpretable and local model behaviors. We dig in a small transformer trained on a synthetic task named Othello and find a number of human-understandable fine-grained circuits inside of it.

7. DLM-Scope: Mechanistic Interpretability of Diffusion Language Models via Sparse Autoencoders (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a standard tool for mechanistic interpretability in autoregressive large language models (LLMs), enabling researchers to extract sparse, human-interpretable features and intervene on model behavior. Recently, as diffusion language models (DLMs) have become an increasingly promising alternative to the autoregressive LLMs, it is essential to develop tailored mechanistic interpretability tools for this emerging class of models. In this work, we present DLM-Scope, the first SAE-based interpretability framework for DLMs, and demonstrate that trained Top-K SAEs can faithfully extract interpretable features. Notably, we find that inserting SAEs affects DLMs differently than autoregressive LLMs: while SAE insertion in LLMs typically incurs a loss penalty, in DLMs it can reduce cross-entropy loss when applied to early layers, a phenomenon absent or markedly weaker in LLMs. Additionally, SAE features in DLMs enable more effective diffusion-time interventions, often outperforming LLM steering. Moreover, we pioneer certain new SAE-based research directions for DLMs: we show that SAEs can provide useful signals for DLM decoding order; and the SAE features are stable during the post-training phase of DLMs. Our work establishes a foundation for mechanistic interpretability in DLMs and shows a great potential of applying SAEs to DLM-related tasks and algorithms.

8. How does Chain of Thought Think? Mechanistic Interpretability of Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Sparse Autoencoding (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2025, cites: 9)
   Chain‑of‑thought (CoT) prompting boosts Large Language Models accuracy on multi‑step tasks, yet whether the generated ``thoughts'' reflect the true internal reasoning process is unresolved. We present the first feature‑level causal study of CoT faithfulness. Combining sparse autoencoders with activation patching, we extract monosemantic features from Pythia‑70M and Pythia‑2.8B while they tackle GSM8K math problems under CoT and plain (noCoT) prompting. Swapping a small set of CoT‑reasoning features into a noCoT run raises answer log‑probabilities significantly in the 2.8B model, but has no reliable effect in 70M, revealing a clear contrast for these two scales. CoT also leads to significantly higher activation sparsity and feature interpretability scores in the larger model, signalling more modular internal computation. For example, the model's confidence in generating correct answers improves from 1.2 to 4.3. We introduce patch‑curves and random‑feature patching baselines, showing that useful CoT information is not only present in the top-K patches but widely distributed. Overall, our results indicate that CoT can induce more interpretable internal structures in high-capacity LLMs, validating its role as a structured prompting method.

9. Mechanistic Interpretability of Code Correctness in LLMs via Sparse Autoencoders (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   As Large Language Models become integral to software development, with substantial portions of AI-suggested code entering production, understanding their internal correctness mechanisms becomes critical for safe deployment. We apply sparse autoencoders to decompose LLM representations, identifying directions that correspond to code correctness. We select predictor directions using t-statistics and steering directions through separation scores from base model representations, then analyze their mechanistic properties through steering, attention analysis, and weight orthogonalization. We find that code correctness directions in LLMs reliably predict incorrect code, while correction capabilities, though statistically significant, involve tradeoffs between fixing errors and preserving correct code. Mechanistically, successful code generation depends on attending to test cases rather than problem descriptions. Moreover, directions identified in base models retain their effectiveness after instruction-tuning, suggesting code correctness mechanisms learned during pre-training are repurposed during fine-tuning. Our mechanistic insights suggest three practical applications: prompting strategies should prioritize test examples over elaborate problem descriptions, predictor directions can serve as error alarms for developer review, and these same predictors can guide selective steering, intervening only when errors are anticipated to prevent the code corruption from constant steering.

10. Position: Mechanistic Interpretability Should Prioritize Feature Consistency in SAEs (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 8)
   Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are a prominent tool in mechanistic interpretability (MI) for decomposing neural network activations into interpretable features. However, the aspiration to identify a canonical set of features is challenged by the observed inconsistency of learned SAE features across different training runs, undermining the reliability and efficiency of MI research. This position paper argues that mechanistic interpretability should prioritize feature consistency in SAEs -- the reliable convergence to equivalent feature sets across independent runs. We propose using the Pairwise Dictionary Mean Correlation Coefficient (PW-MCC) as a practical metric to operationalize consistency and demonstrate that high levels are achievable (0.80 for TopK SAEs on LLM activations) with appropriate architectural choices. Our contributions include detailing the benefits of prioritizing consistency; providing theoretical grounding and synthetic validation using a model organism, which verifies PW-MCC as a reliable proxy for ground-truth recovery; and extending these findings to real-world LLM data, where high feature consistency strongly correlates with the semantic similarity of learned feature explanations. We call for a community-wide shift towards systematically measuring feature consistency to foster robust cumulative progress in MI.
